Data Overview & Exploration

TCR Repertoire Analysis — Notebook 01

Joshua Luthy

2026-03-15

Dataset Overview

This notebook provides initial exploration of the synthetic TCR repertoire dataset. The data simulates a cell therapy manufacturing workflow where PBMCs from the apheresis collection are processed into a final drug product.

tcr_data <- read_csv("../data/raw/synthetic_tcr_repertoires.csv")
patient_meta <- read_csv("../data/raw/patient_metadata.csv")

n_patients    <- n_distinct(tcr_data$patient_id)
n_samples     <- n_distinct(paste(tcr_data$patient_id, tcr_data$sample_type))
n_clonotypes  <- nrow(tcr_data)
n_reads       <- sum(tcr_data$clone_count)
6
Patients
12
Samples
81,593
Total Clonotypes
1,125,178
Total Reads
glimpse(tcr_data)

Patient Cohort

The cohort includes 6 AML patients with varying clinical responses to cell therapy: 2 complete responders (CR), 2 partial responders (PR), and 2 with progressive disease (PD).

patient_meta %>%
  knitr::kable(caption = "Patient cohort summary")
Patient cohort summary
patient_id clinical_response age sex disease prior_lines
PT-001 CR 60 F AML 2
PT-002 CR 46 M AML 2
PT-003 PR 43 M AML 3
PT-004 PR 59 M AML 4
PT-005 PD 47 F AML 2
PT-006 PD 58 F AML 2

Per-Sample Summary

For each patient, we have paired Apheresis (starting material) and Product (manufactured drug product) TCR repertoire samples.

# Custom Shannon entropy (avoids vegan dependency for portability)
shannon_entropy <- function(counts) {
  p <- counts / sum(counts)
  p <- p[p > 0]
  -sum(p * log(p))
}

diversity_summary <- tcr_data %>%
  group_by(patient_id, sample_type, clinical_response) %>%
  summarise(
    n_clonotypes    = n_distinct(clonotype_id),
    total_reads     = sum(clone_count),
    shannon_entropy = shannon_entropy(clone_count),
    clonality       = 1 - (shannon_entropy / log(n_clonotypes)),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

diversity_summary %>%
  mutate(across(c(shannon_entropy, clonality), ~round(., 4))) %>%
  knitr::kable(
    caption = "Diversity metrics per sample",
    format.args = list(big.mark = ",")
  )
Diversity metrics per sample
patient_id sample_type clinical_response n_clonotypes total_reads shannon_entropy clonality
PT-001 Apheresis CR 13,238 94,857 7.9996 0.1571
PT-001 Product CR 2,029 72,517 4.4388 0.4171
PT-002 Apheresis CR 14,132 106,246 8.1066 0.1517
PT-002 Product CR 940 79,693 3.0637 0.5525
PT-003 Apheresis PR 11,658 120,934 7.7771 0.1694
PT-003 Product PR 1,123 99,121 3.4408 0.5101
PT-004 Apheresis PR 12,786 126,170 7.9188 0.1626
PT-004 Product PR 2,474 51,822 4.8667 0.3771
PT-005 Apheresis PD 9,771 144,336 7.4659 0.1874
PT-005 Product PD 1,913 60,767 4.6019 0.3910
PT-006 Apheresis PD 8,676 113,231 7.2834 0.1968
PT-006 Product PD 2,853 55,484 5.2527 0.3398

Key Observation: Apheresis samples show high diversity (Shannon H > 7.0, low clonality) with thousands of unique clonotypes, while Product samples are dramatically more oligoclonal (Shannon H 3.0–5.3, clonality 0.34–0.55). This is consistent with the expected biology: manufacturing selects and expands a subset of T cell clones from the starting material.

V Gene Usage

We examine TRBV gene segment usage across samples to identify preferential gene usage patterns between Apheresis and Product.

vgene_summary <- tcr_data %>%
  group_by(sample_type, v_gene) %>%
  summarise(total_count = sum(clone_count), .groups = "drop") %>%
  group_by(sample_type) %>%
  mutate(fraction = total_count / sum(total_count)) %>%
  slice_max(fraction, n = 10) %>%
  ungroup()

ggplot(vgene_summary, aes(x = reorder(v_gene, fraction), y = fraction,
                           fill = sample_type)) +
  geom_col(position = "dodge", alpha = 0.8, width = 0.7) +
  coord_flip() +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("Apheresis" = "#00d4ff", "Product" = "#7b2fff")) +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "Read Fraction", fill = "Sample Type",
       title = "Top 10 TRBV Gene Segments",
       subtitle = "Read fraction by sample type, aggregated across all patients")

Data Quality Notes

Synthetic Data Disclaimer: This dataset is entirely synthetic, generated to demonstrate analytical methods and pipeline architecture. CDR3 sequences are randomly generated and do not correspond to real TCR rearrangements. Clone size distributions follow power-law models calibrated to approximate real repertoire characteristics.

Data quality checks:

  • All CDR3 sequences begin with C and end with F (conserved anchors) ✓
  • Clone fractions sum to ~1.0 within each sample ✓
  • No duplicate clonotype IDs within samples ✓
  • All patients have paired Apheresis + Product samples ✓
  • TRBV/TRBJ gene names match IMGT nomenclature ✓
